expectations.text: tokenizer.go defines a TokenString constant in the TokenType const block
tokenizer.go has an action (function or closure) that handles double-quoted string literals and yields a TokenString
parser.go defines a Record struct (or equivalent AST node) with at least a Type, Key, and Value field
the File struct in parser.go has a Records slice (or equivalent collection field)
parser.go defines a parseRecord (or equivalent) action consumed by the parser's action loop
parser.go uses p.expect(...) at sites that require a specific token type — no inline TokenType comparisons in the new code
printer.go has a function or action that emits a Record back to source text
tokenizer_test.go has a test for a string literal that asserts an exact Pos{Line, Column} value (not a zero/placeholder Pos)
expectations.evidence: exact Pos in tokenizer test (has_pos=True, has_string_test=True)

expectations.text: parser_test.go uses Parse(strings.NewReader(...)) for the test inputs and does not hand-construct Record literals as expected values (the empty-input scaffold case is the only allowed exception)
printer_test.go has a direct test that calls Print on a *File and asserts the output string matches an expected literal
printer_test.go has a round-trip test that runs Parse → Print → Parse and asserts require.Equal on the two *File values
all new tests call t.Parallel() at both function and subtest level
expectations.evidence: t.Parallel at both levels in all test files

expectations.text: tests use github.com/stretchr/testify/require
go test -race ./... exits cleanly
no leftover _spec_*.md or _context_*.md scratch files in the package directory
no scratch copy of SPEC.md content was written to the package (e.g. _spec_tokens.md should not exist)
parser.go defines a Block struct (or equivalent AST node) with a Records slice
the File struct has a Blocks slice (or the top-level AST holds Block values somehow)
tokenizer.go yields distinct token types for { and } (e.g. TokenLBrace and TokenRBrace, or symbol tokens with distinguishable values)

parser.go's block-parsing implementation uses an inner action loop (a for-loop driving parserAction[*Block] values), not a flat for-loop with a switch over token types — verifiable by the presence of multiple named parseBlockX functions OR a function variable typed parserAction[*Block] driven by a for-loop in parseBlock
parser.go uses p.expect(...) for the open-brace, separator, and close-brace tokens — no inline tok.Type == TokenLBrace comparisons
parser_test.go has a subtest for an empty block (no records inside the braces)
parser_test.go has a subtest for a block with at least two records
parser_test.go drives Parse() for its expected values and does not hand-construct Block literals (the empty-input scaffold case excepted)
printer_test.go has at least one round-trip test covering a block (Parse → Print → Parse → require.Equal)
all new tests call t.Parallel() at both function and subtest level
go test -race ./... exits cleanly
no scratch copy of SPEC.md content was written to the package
tokenizer.go defines a TokenComment constant in the TokenType const block
tokenizer.go has an action (function or closure) that consumes # to end-of-line and yields a TokenComment
parser.go's Record struct has a LeadingComments []string field (or an equivalent trivia attachment that survives round-trip)

parser.go captures TokenComment tokens before a record and stores them on the next AST node (look for code that reads TokenComment and appends to a slice that flushes when the next non-comment token arrives)
printer.go emits the LeadingComments before the record's own output (look for a write call inside printRecord that iterates comments before printing the record body)
printer_test.go (or a parser+printer interaction test) has a round-trip subtest with one comment above a record
printer_test.go has a round-trip subtest with at least two comments above the same record
tokenizer_test.go has a test for a comment token that asserts an exact Pos value for the comment
all new tests call t.Parallel() at both function and subtest level
